title: Add BehaviorMap disk cache in shatter-core
description: BehaviorMaps are currently in-memory only. Add a BehaviorMapCache abstraction that can serialize/deserialize BehaviorMaps to a directory on disk, keyed by function_id. Default cache location should be .shatter/cache/ relative to the target project root. Cache should be the default behavior (not opt-in). The cache module should handle: writing a BehaviorMap to disk after it's built from execution records, loading a cached BehaviorMap if one exists for a function_id, invalidating/overwriting stale entries when new records are collected. File format: JSON, one file per function (e.g. .shatter/cache/getDiscount.json). Additionally, support a --cache-dir <path> CLI argument and SHATTER_CACHE_DIR environment variable to override the default cache directory. The CLI flag takes precedence over the env var, which takes precedence over the default. The path can be absolute (e.g. /tmp/shatter-cache) or relative to CWD.

title: Mirror source tree structure in .shatter/cache/ directory
description:
The current cache uses a flat namespace: `.shatter/cache/<sanitized_function_id>.json`. This will collide when two files contain functions with the same name (e.g. `src/auth.ts:validate` and `src/payments.ts:validate` both become `validate.json`).

## Proposed structure

Mirror the source tree, keeping file extensions to avoid collisions between e.g. `utils.ts` and `utils.js`:

```
.shatter/cache/
  src/
    auth.ts/                       # source file becomes directory (keep extension)
      validateToken.json           # free function
      TokenValidator/              # class becomes subdirectory
        validate.json              # method
        refresh.json
    payments/
      process.ts/
        calculateTotal.json
        getDiscount.json
```

## Mapping rules

- Source file path becomes a directory path, extension included
- Free functions are files directly inside the file directory
- Classes become subdirectories; methods are files inside them
- Target `src/auth.ts:validateToken` → `src/auth.ts/validateToken.json`
- Target `src/auth.ts:TokenValidator.validate` → `src/auth.ts/TokenValidator/validate.json`
- Nested classes (`Outer.Inner.method`) → `Outer/Inner/method.json`

## Implementation

- Update `BehaviorMapCache::path_for()` in `cache.rs` to split the function_id on `:` (file vs function) and `.` (class vs method) to build the directory path
- `sanitize_filename()` still needed for individual path components but no longer flattens the entire ID
- `load()` and `store()` unchanged in interface — only `path_for()` changes
- `store()` already does atomic write via temp file + rename; ensure the parent directory is created before writing

## Migration

- On first run after upgrade, old flat cache files are ignored (cache miss, function gets re-explored)
- No explicit migration needed — re-exploration is cheap and the old files are harmless
- Could optionally log a warning if flat files are detected and suggest clearing the cache

## Why keep the file extension

Stripping extensions would cause collisions: `utils.ts` and `utils.js` in the same directory would both map to `utils/`. All modern operating systems allow dots in directory names.

title: Update README CLI reference to document all commands
description: README.md CLI Reference section only documents 'explore' with 4 flags. The CLI has 6 commands (explore, scan, export-tests, run, diff, spec-diff) and explore alone has 19 flags. At minimum add a link to SPEC.md or document all commands.

title: Genetic/evolutionary input generation (optional, persisted)
description:
Add an optional genetic/evolutionary search mode for generating candidate inputs where the constraint solver fails (opaque predicates, hash comparisons, complex string matching, floating point).

## Motivation

Z3 cannot solve all constraints. When a branch condition involves a hash check, checksum validation, or other opaque computation, the solver returns unknown/unsat. Currently these branches remain uncovered. A genetic algorithm can hill-climb toward coverage of these branches using mutation, crossover, and coverage feedback — targeting specific unsolved partitions rather than blindly maximizing overall coverage (which is EvoSuite's weakness, generating many redundant inputs).

## Key design decisions

### Optional and explicit
- NOT part of the default exploration flow
- Activated via `--genetic` CLI flag or `genetic.enabled: true` in `.shatter/config.yaml`
- Expected to run occasionally (nightly, weekly, or manually) not on every commit
- Has its own iteration budget separate from the main exploration budget

### Persisted results
- Any test vector discovered by the genetic search that covers a new equivalence class gets persisted to the behavior map cache, exactly like solver-generated inputs
- Subsequent runs see these as cached behaviors — no need to re-run the genetic search
- The genetic search is a one-time cost whose value compounds across all future runs

### Targeted, not blind
- Only activated for equivalence classes where Z3 returned unknown/unsat
- Receives the unsolved path condition as a fitness hint (get closer to satisfying this)
- Uses coverage feedback from the frontend to measure progress
- Stops early if it finds a witness for the target partition

## Algorithm sketch
1. Seed population from existing concrete inputs (from prior exploration runs)
2. Mutate: type-aware perturbation (bit flips for ints, char substitution for strings, field shuffling for objects)
3. Crossover: combine fields from two parents
4. Evaluate: execute each candidate, measure branch distance to target
5. Select: tournament selection favoring candidates closer to the target branch
6. Repeat until witness found or budget exhausted
7. Persist any new behaviors discovered

## Configuration
- `genetic.enabled` (bool, default false) — master toggle
- `genetic.population_size` (u32, default 50) — candidates per generation
- `genetic.max_generations` (u32, default 100) — iteration budget
- `genetic.mutation_rate` (f64, default 0.3) — probability of mutating each field
- `genetic.crossover_rate` (f64, default 0.7) — probability of crossover vs clone
- `genetic.timeout` (u32, default 300) — total seconds budget

## Relation to other input strategies
Sits between dictionary values and LLM-guided generation in priority. Expensive but handles opaque constraints that cheaper strategies cannot.

## Prior art
- EvoSuite (Java): genetic algorithm for JUnit test generation
- AFL/libFuzzer: coverage-guided mutation on byte streams
- FUZZOLIC: hybrid concolic + fuzzing

title: Support mutual recursion in compositional testing
description:
Extend recursive function support to handle mutually recursive function groups (e.g. is_even/is_odd, parser combinators).

## Context

Self-recursion (f calls f) is handled by iterative deepening with self-call mocking. Mutual recursion (f calls g, g calls f) requires bootstrapping the entire cycle simultaneously.

## Strategy

1. Detect strongly connected components (SCCs) in the call graph. Each SCC with >1 node is a mutual recursion group.
2. For each function in the group, identify base-case branches (branches that don't call any other function in the group).
3. Bootstrap: solve for base-case inputs across ALL functions in the group simultaneously, building initial BehaviorMaps for each.
4. Iterative deepening: at each depth, mock cross-calls within the group using BehaviorMaps from depth ≤ k. Explore all functions in the group at each depth.
5. The group's BehaviorMaps grow together. Stop when coverage stabilizes across all functions or max depth is reached.

## Implementation

1. Replace simple cycle detection in CallGraph with SCC detection (Tarjan's or Kosaraju's algorithm).
2. Single-node SCCs with a self-edge → self-recursive (handled by existing iterative deepening).
3. Multi-node SCCs → mutual recursion groups. Process as a unit in test_order().
4. Extend the orchestrator's deepening loop to handle groups: at each depth, explore each function in the group with mocks for all other functions in the group.

## Example: is_even(n) / is_odd(n)

- Base cases: is_even(0)→true, is_odd(0)→false
- Depth 1: is_even(1) calls is_odd(0)→false from mock → is_even(1)→false; is_odd(1) calls is_even(0)→true from mock → is_odd(1)→true
- Depth 2: is_even(2) calls is_odd(1)→true → is_even(2)→true; etc.

## Scope

This is lower priority than self-recursion support since mutual recursion is less common in practice. Most mutual recursion involves small cycles (2-3 functions). Large cycles may need special handling or manual annotation.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — CallGraph, test_order()
- shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs — explore loop

## Tests

- is_even/is_odd: mutual recursion detected, base cases found, BehaviorMaps built correctly
- Three-function cycle: A→B→C→A, all base cases found
- Mixed graph: some functions self-recursive, some mutually recursive, some neither — all handled correctly
close_reason: Implemented mutual recursion support: Tarjan's SCC algorithm in CallGraph, MutualGroup variant in TestOrderEntry, explore_mutual_group() for group-level iterative deepening.

This is the hardest part of Rust support — the type system is significantly richer than TS or Go.

title: Iterative deepening via self-call mocking
description:
Implement the core recursive function testing strategy: iteratively build a BehaviorMap by mocking self-calls at increasing depth.

## Context

Once base-case inputs are known (from branch analysis or small-value probing), we can test recursive functions compositionally by treating self-calls the same way we treat calls to other functions — mock them using the BehaviorMap built so far.

## Algorithm

1. Start with the base-case BehaviorMap (depth 0): inputs that don't trigger any recursive call.
2. For depth k+1:
   a. Configure mocks for self-calls using the BehaviorMap from depth ≤ k
   b. Explore the function — the solver targets recursive branches
   c. When a recursive branch is taken, the self-call is intercepted and returns a mocked result from the existing BehaviorMap
   d. New input→output pairs discovered at this depth are added to the BehaviorMap
3. Repeat until: coverage stabilizes (no new branches discovered), or max depth reached.
4. The final BehaviorMap is used by callers of this function, same as any other function.

## Implementation

1. Add a max_recursion_depth field to ExploreConfig (default: 10).
2. In the orchestrator, when processing a self-recursive function:
   a. Run base-case exploration first (constraint solve for non-recursive branches + probing)
   b. Enter the iterative deepening loop
   c. At each depth, create a MockConfig from the current BehaviorMap and configure it for self-calls
   d. Track coverage across depths; stop when no new behaviors are found
3. The BehaviorMap grows incrementally. Each depth adds new entries.

## Example: factorial(n)

- Depth 0: base case n≤1 → BehaviorMap = {0→1, 1→1}
- Depth 1: mock factorial(n-1) using depth-0 map → try n=2, gets factorial(1)=1 from mock → BehaviorMap adds {2→2}
- Depth 2: try n=3, gets factorial(2)=2 from mock → BehaviorMap adds {3→6}
- Coverage stabilizes (both branches covered at depth 1), but we continue to configured max depth for richer behavior map.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs — ExploreConfig, explore loop
- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — BehaviorMap, MockConfig, CompositeResult

## Tests

- factorial(n): iterative deepening builds correct BehaviorMap up to configured depth
- fibonacci(n): two recursive calls per invocation, BehaviorMap grows correctly
- Coverage stabilization: deepening stops when no new branches are found
- Max depth cutoff: deepening stops at configured limit even if new behaviors exist
close_reason: Implemented iterative deepening via self-call mocking in recursive.rs. explore_recursive() probes for base cases, then iteratively deepens by mocking self-calls with BehaviorMap, stopping when coverage stabilizes or max_depth reached.

title: TS frontend: missing taken field for rest-parameter functions
description:
DUPLICATE OF str-o7a.

Root cause confirmed: JSON.stringify({taken: undefined}) produces {} — the field is omitted entirely. When a branch condition evaluates to undefined in JS (possible with various expressions including those involving rest parameters, prototype manipulation, etc.), the taken field disappears from the serialized JSON, producing 'missing field taken' in the core deserializer.

Fix: same as str-o7a — coerce conditionResult to boolean via Boolean() or !! in either instrumentor.ts (wrapBranchCondition) or executor.ts (branchFn line 328: taken: Boolean(conditionResult)).

Original es-toolkit partialImpl reproduction case should be added as a test case for the str-o7a fix.
design:
## Investigation Notes

The original hypothesis (rest parameters cause taken to be omitted) is likely wrong. The branchFn in executor.ts sets taken unconditionally on every branch callback invocation. The BranchDecision struct in execution_record.rs requires taken: bool with no serde(default).

## Probable Root Causes (to investigate)

1. **Exception during instrumented execution aborts before branches collected**: If the instrumented code throws before any branch is hit, branch_path could be empty (which is fine, it has serde(default)). But if the thrown error somehow produces a partial BranchDecision (missing taken), that would cause this error.

2. **The 'taken' error might not be from BranchDecision at all**: The column number in the error message ('column 481') could point to a different struct. Other structs with boolean fields that could produce 'missing field taken':
   - BranchStep in equivalence.rs also has a 'taken: bool' field
   - Need to check if the core constructs BranchStep from the response and whether the mapping could lose the field

3. **The real issue might be the boolean coercion bug (str-o7a)**: If the condition evaluates to undefined/null for a rest-parameter function, and the JSON serializes as {taken: null}, the error message would be 'invalid type: null, expected a boolean' not 'missing field taken'. BUT if JavaScript omits undefined values during JSON.stringify, then taken: undefined would be OMITTED entirely, producing 'missing field taken'.

## Investigation Plan

1. Reproduce with es-toolkit partialImpl locally
2. Intercept the raw JSON response from the frontend (add stderr logging before core parses it)
3. Identify which struct and field the 'missing field taken at column 481' refers to
4. Trace the execution path to find where taken becomes undefined

## Relationship to str-o7a

If the root cause is taken: undefined being omitted by JSON.stringify, then this IS the same bug as str-o7a (boolean coercion), just a different manifestation. The fix would be the same: Boolean() coercion in the branchFn or instrumentor.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Reproduce the error with partialImpl
- Identify the exact root cause (is taken undefined, null, or something else?)
- Confirm whether this is a duplicate of str-o7a or a separate issue
- Fix and verify the function explores successfully
close_reason: Duplicate of str-o7a. Root cause: JSON.stringify omits undefined values, so taken: undefined becomes a missing field. Same boolean coercion fix applies.

title: Built-in dictionary of common boundary values by type
description:
Ship a pre-configured dictionary of values known to trigger boundary conditions and common bugs, organized by type. These supplement solver-generated and code-extracted candidates.

## Motivation
Many bugs cluster around well-known boundary values that are universal across codebases — epoch boundaries for dates, MAX_INT for integers, empty strings, null bytes, etc. A developer shouldn't need to rediscover these. The dictionary provides baseline coverage of classic edge cases without any code analysis.

## Example values by type

### Dates/timestamps
- 1969-12-31T23:59:59Z (one second before Unix epoch)
- 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z (Unix epoch)
- 2000-01-01, 2000-02-29 (Y2K, leap day)
- 2038-01-19T03:14:07Z (32-bit overflow)
- 9999-12-31 (max representable in many systems)

### Numbers (integers)
- 0, -1, 1
- MIN_INT, MAX_INT (for 8/16/32/64 bit)
- Powers of 2 and their neighbors (255/256/257, 65535/65536)
- NaN, Infinity, -Infinity (floats)
- 0.1 + 0.2 neighborhood (IEEE 754 precision)

### Strings
- empty string, single char, very long string
- null byte embedded ("\x00")
- Unicode edge cases (BOM, RTL marks, emoji, combining chars)
- SQL/XSS/path traversal payloads (for security-sensitive contexts)
- Strings that look like numbers ("0", "NaN", "null", "undefined")

### Collections
- empty, single element, two elements
- duplicate elements
- already sorted, reverse sorted

### Booleans and nulls
- true, false, null, undefined
- 0 and "" as falsy values (JS-specific)

## Implementation
- Core ships a built-in dictionary as a static data structure (not a file to load)
- Dictionary entries are tagged with type and category (boundary, security, unicode, etc.)
- Frontends can extend with language-specific values (e.g., JS falsy values, Go zero values)
- When the explorer encounters a parameter of a known type, it pulls relevant dictionary entries as initial candidates
- Dictionary values are lowest priority — solver-generated and code-extracted values take precedence

## Relation to other input sources
This is one of several candidate input strategies:
1. Constraint solver (Z3) — highest priority, mathematically precise
2. Code-extracted literals — from the codebase itself
3. Dictionary values — universal boundary conditions (this issue)
4. Genetic/evolutionary — fallback for opaque constraints
5. LLM-guided — semantic understanding
6. Random — last resort

title: TS frontend: branch condition not coerced to boolean, causing deserialization failures
description:
The TS frontend's instrumentor passes the raw condition expression to __shatter_branch() without Boolean() coercion (instrumentor.ts wrapBranchCondition() lines 691-710). At runtime, truthy/falsy non-booleans (numbers, null, strings) are assigned to the 'taken' field, which the core expects to be boolean. This produces 'invalid type: null, expected a boolean' and 'invalid type: floating point, expected a boolean' errors.

Also covers the TypeScript 'asserts' return type case — asserts condition functions evaluate to truthy non-boolean values that get serialized as-is.

Fix (either one):
1. instrumentor.ts wrapBranchCondition(): wrap condition arg with !! in the generated AST
2. executor.ts branchFn (~line 325): taken: Boolean(conditionResult)

Option 1 is preferred (fix at generation site). Affects change-case splitSeparateNumbers, es-toolkit invariant, and others.

**Requirement: Reproduction test first.** Before attempting any fix, write an automated test that reproduces the bug (demonstrating the deserialization failure when a non-boolean condition value is encountered). The test must fail before the fix and pass after.
close_reason: Fixed with !! coercion in instrumentor.ts wrapBranchCondition()

6. Integration test: isWeekend(date: Date): boolean — function with two paths (weekday/weekend), verify solver generates dates hitting both paths

## Solver
ConstValue::Complex { kind: Date, repr: Int(epoch_ms) } — solver unwraps to integer comparison. Already handled by the generic Complex unwrap from str-t8b.

## Priority
Highest real-world impact — Date appears in nearly every business application.

## Files
- shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs
- shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts
- shatter-ts/src/reconstruct.ts (new)
- shatter-ts/src/executor.ts (call reconstructValue before execution)

- examples/typescript/src/ (isWeekend test function)

title: TS frontend: intra-package module resolution fails (Cannot find module)
description:
The TS frontend uses ts.transpileModule per-file + vm.createContext with the host process's require. Relative imports to sibling modules within the target package fail with 'Cannot find module'. This is the #1 error source — 365 of 366 total errors across all TS holdout targets. Causes complete failure for ts-pattern (0/35 functions) and partial failure for remeda (48/561) and es-toolkit.

Root cause: there is no bundling step. Target files are transpiled individually and executed in a VM sandbox. Relative require() calls fail because module resolution doesn't account for the target project's directory structure.

Design questions that need answering before implementation:
- How does bundling interact with per-function instrumentation?
- Would bundling break the per-function isolation model?
- How to handle tsconfig.json paths/aliases?
- Should we bundle at analysis time, execution time, or both?

Needs design work before implementation.

**Requirement: Reproduction test first.** Before attempting any fix, write an automated test that reproduces the bug (demonstrating the 'Cannot find module' failure for relative imports in the VM sandbox). The test must fail before the fix and pass after.
design:
## Root Cause

The TS frontend uses ts.transpileModule per-file + vm.createContext with the **host process's require**. Three distinct bugs:

1. **executeInstrumented** (executor.ts ~line 289): sandbox is missing __filename and __dirname entirely, and uses host require. The vm.runInContext filename is 'instrumented.js' (fake).
2. **loadModule** (executor.ts ~line 69): sets __dirname correctly but still uses the host require — so require('./sibling') resolves against shatter-ts install dir.
3. **loadAndTranspile** (setup-loader.ts ~line 45): same issue as loadModule.

The instrumented code preserves all imports (ts.transpileModule with CommonJS converts them to require() calls), so require('./purry') etc. always fails.

## Fix Design

Use Node.js `module.createRequire(filename)` (stable since Node 12) to create a require anchored to the target file's directory.

### Changes Required

**executor.ts:**
- Add `import { createRequire } from 'node:module'`
- loadModule: replace `require` with `createRequire(absolutePath)` in sandbox
- executeInstrumented: add `sourceFilePath: string` parameter; use `createRequire(sourceFilePath)` + set __filename/__dirname in sandbox; pass sourceFilePath to vm.runInContext filename option; add `fileName: sourceFilePath` to ts.transpileModule options

**handlers.ts:**
- Pass `fileForExec` (already resolved at line ~161) as 5th argument to executeInstrumented

**setup-loader.ts:**
- Add `import { createRequire } from 'node:module'`
- Replace host require with `createRequire(absolutePath)` in loadAndTranspile sandbox

### Test Plan
- Existing fixture `__fixtures__/dependencies.ts` imports from `./deps-helper` — verify it works after fix
- Add integration test: target file importing sibling module in a temp directory outside shatter-ts
- Verify ts-pattern-style scenarios (deep internal imports like './internals/helpers')
close_reason: Fixed with module.createRequire() in executor.ts, handlers.ts, setup-loader.ts

description:
Allow users to declare custom opaque types in .shatter/config.yaml so internal/third-party resource types not in the built-in lists are detected.

## What
1. shatter-core/src/config.rs — add opaque_types: Vec<String> field to project-level config
2. Frontend analyzers check the config list in addition to built-in detection. If a type's fully-qualified name matches an entry in opaque_types, emit Opaque instead of Unknown/Object.
3. Config format:
   opaque_types:
     - "com.company.internal.DbWrapper"
     - "MyCustomSocket"
     - "cloud.storage.BlobClient"

## Why
Built-in lists cover common stdlib types but can't anticipate every internal or third-party resource type. User config is the escape hatch — no code changes needed for custom types.

## Tests
- Config parsing: opaque_types list is read correctly
- Empty list is valid (default)
- Frontend respects config: function with parameter matching opaque_types entry emits Opaque

title: Core: ConstValue::Complex, ConcreteValue::Complex, and solver unwrap
description:
Extend the symbolic expression and constraint solver to handle complex type constants.

## What
1. Add ConstValue::Complex { kind: ComplexKind, repr: Box<ConstValue> } to shatter-core/src/sym_expr.rs
2. Add ConcreteValue::Complex { kind: ComplexKind, repr: Box<ConcreteValue> } to shatter-core/src/solver.rs
3. Handle ConstValue::Complex in the solver's to_z3_expr by unwrapping repr and recursing

## Why
When a frontend instruments code like `if (date > new Date("2024-01-01"))`, it needs to emit a constant that preserves the complex type identity while carrying an underlying primitive the solver can reason about. For dates, the repr is Int(epoch_millis); for bigint, Str(digit_string); for char, Int(codepoint). The solver unwraps repr and reasons about the primitive directly — no Z3 changes needed for Tier 1 solvable types.

## Design
- ConstValue::Complex { kind, repr } where repr is the underlying value Z3 reasons about
- ConcreteValue::Complex { kind, repr } for solved values that need to be tagged in the wire format
- Solver handles Complex by recursing: ConstValue::Complex { repr, .. } => to_z3_const(repr, hint_sort)

## Files
- shatter-core/src/sym_expr.rs — ConstValue::Complex variant
- shatter-core/src/solver.rs — ConcreteValue::Complex variant + to_z3_expr handling

## Tests
- Round-trip serialization for ConstValue::Complex with various repr types (Int, Str, Float)
- Round-trip serialization for ConcreteValue::Complex
- Solver correctly unwraps Complex(Date, Int(epoch)) in comparisons
- Solver returns Complex(Date, Int(solved_epoch)) in results
close_reason: ConstValue::Complex added to sym_expr.rs with kind+repr design. ConcreteValue::Complex added to solver.rs. Solver unwraps Complex repr for Z3 solving. concrete_to_json handles Complex. All 406 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Make-friendly per-file specs with fingerprint-based incremental exploration
description:
## Problem

Shatter's CLI currently bundles discovery, analysis, and exploration into monolithic commands (scan, run) with outputs going to hidden .shatter/cache/ or shatter-report/. This makes it unusable with Make, which needs predictable input→output file relationships with timestamp-based staleness.

The lasting output of Shatter is behavioral specifications — not human reports. Specs are the precise, complete, machine-diffable records of observed behavior. They should be first-class project artifacts, not hidden cache.

## Design

### Two first-class artifacts, per-source-file granularity

1. **Spec files** (FunctionSpec[]) — the lasting, diffable behavioral specification. Versioned in git. Used by spec-diff for regression detection.
2. **Behavior map files** (BehaviorMap[]) — input→output mappings for compositional mocking. Stored separately, may or may not be versioned.

Both are per-source-file: specs/src/math.ts.json contains specs for all functions in math.ts.

### Internal function fingerprinting for performance

When a source file changes, Shatter doesn't blindly re-explore all functions:
1. Run cheap analyze phase → extract function signatures/ASTs
2. Compute per-function fingerprint (hash of source text, parameter types, branch structure)
3. Compare against fingerprints stored in existing output
4. Only re-explore functions with changed fingerprints
5. Merge results: preserve unchanged specs, update changed ones

Fingerprints should compose across the call graph: if B's behavior changes, A (which calls B) needs re-exploration even if A's source is unchanged.

### CLI changes needed

1. **explore --output <path>** — write spec to explicit path (Make target)
2. **explore accepts file-level targets** — shatter explore src/math.ts explores all functions in the file
3. **shatter stale** — lightweight staleness check (exit code 0/1) using fingerprints, for non-Make incremental workflows
4. **explore --clean** — ignore existing specs/fingerprints and force full re-exploration from scratch. Escape hatch for when fingerprints are stale, cache is corrupted, or exploration heuristics have changed.
5. **explore --dry-run** — run analyze + fingerprint comparison, print what would be explored (which functions are stale, which would be skipped), but don't actually run the concolic engine. Useful for understanding what Make would trigger, debugging fingerprint behavior, and CI cost estimation.

### Makefile usage

```makefile
SPECS_DIR := specs

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/math.ts.json: src/math.ts
    shatter explore src/math.ts -o $@

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/utils.ts.json: src/utils.ts
    shatter explore src/utils.ts -o $@
```

### What stays the same

- scan and run remain as batteries-included commands for users not using Make
- .shatter/cache/ remains for internal caching (behavior maps for compositional mocking during exploration)
- export-tests unchanged — reads from specs or cache

### Key files that will need changes

- shatter-core/src/cache.rs — fingerprint storage, staleness checks
- shatter-core/src/spec.rs — per-file spec bundles (array of FunctionSpec)
- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — per-file behavior map bundles
- shatter-cli/src/main.rs — --output flag, --clean flag, --dry-run flag, file-level targets, stale command
- shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs — fingerprint-aware incremental exploration

title: Rust frontend: code instrumentor
description: Implement the Instrument command handler that rewrites Rust source code to inject constraint-tracking hooks. For external function calls, insert mock registry lookups that can intercept and replace return values. Use syn for parsing and quote for code generation. Output the instrumented source as a string. The instrumented code must still compile — respect Rust's ownership/borrowing rules in generated code. Consider using a lightweight runtime crate (shatter-rust-runtime) that provides the recording/mock functions.

Exports: (1) branch_hit(id, taken, constraint_json) — records a branch decision, (2) mock_call(symbol, args_json) -> Option<Value> — checks mock registry, returns mock value or None (caller falls through to real impl), (3) record_external_call(symbol, args, return_value) — logs external calls, (4) flush_results() -> ExecuteResult JSON — serializes all recorded data. This crate is added as a dependency of the instrumented code during compilation. Must be thread-safe (use thread_local or Mutex). Keep it minimal — it runs inside the target binary.

title: CLI: Skip unexecutable functions and report reasons
description:
Wire the executability check into the CLI explore and scan commands so functions with opaque parameter types are skipped cleanly and reported.

## What
1. shatter-cli/src/main.rs in run_explore():
   - After existing resolved.skip check (~line 512), call executability::check_executability() on each function
   - Accumulate skipped functions in Vec<(String, Vec<SkipReason>)>
   - After the exploration loop, print summary at INFO level:
     Skipped N function(s) (unexecutable parameter types):
       handleRequest: param "socket" has opaque type net.Socket
       processStream: param "input" has opaque type stream.Readable

2. shatter-cli/src/main.rs in run_scan():
   - Same pattern: check executability before passing functions to scan orchestrator
   - Filter out unexecutable functions, collect skip reasons

3. shatter-core/src/scan_orchestrator.rs:
   - Add skipped_functions field to ScanResult
   - Include "Skipped Functions" section in format_scan_report()

## Why
Currently unexecutable functions crash with garbage inputs, wasting time and producing noisy output. Clean skip + report gives users visibility into what wasn't tested and why.

## Tests
- Integration test: explore a file containing both executable and unexecutable functions, verify only executable ones are explored
- Verify skip summary appears in output

title: TS frontend: symbolic constraint extraction from branches
description: Extend instrumentor.ts to emit symbolic constraints alongside line recording. For each branch condition (if, switch, ternary, &&, ||), analyze the AST to determine if the condition references function parameters. If so, emit a SymExpr representation per the protocol schema. Implement lightweight data flow tracking: when a local variable is assigned from a parameter expression, track the symbolic relationship. Fall back to Unknown for complex flows. acceptance_criteria: For function f(x: number) { if (x > 10) return big; return small; }, the frontend reports branch constraint {kind: binop, op: >, left: {kind: param, name: x}, right: {kind: const, value: 10}}. title: Core: concolic execution loop with Z3
description: Implement the full concolic loop in orchestrator.rs: execute a function with inputs, collect branch constraints, negate each branch constraint and solve with Z3, add solutions to the worklist, repeat. Use rayon to parallelize Z3 solver calls across branches. Implement priority queue ordering (Z3-solved > fuzzed). Include integration test with a simple multi-branch function where random inputs alone cannot find all paths but Z3 can.
acceptance_criteria: Given a function f(x) { if (x === 42) return found; return not found; }, the concolic loop finds the input x = 42 via Z3 solving. Random-only exploration demonstrably fails to find it in reasonable iterations.

title: Explore bounded symbolic execution for array/collection parameters
description:
**This is a future exploration item, not a gap in current functionality.** The current engine already handles arrays through its general concolic loop — it executes concretely, collects per-element branch decisions, negates them with Z3, and falls back to fuzzing for unsolvable constraints. Execution budgets and plateau detection prevent runaway exploration. This works.

The question is whether we can do *better* with array-aware strategies. Sequential processing of collection elements causes exponential path explosion (2^n paths for n elements with one branch each), and the engine currently has no special knowledge that it's exploring array contents vs any other parameter. A smarter approach could focus exploration budget more effectively.

Areas to investigate:

1. **Bounded unrolling** — Recognize array parameters and deliberately generate inputs at lengths 0, 1, 2, 3 rather than random 0-5. Most real bugs appear at small sizes. Spend solving budget on per-element branches at those sizes.

2. **Early-exit path detection** — Identify find/some/indexOf patterns where interesting paths are linear (found at index k, not found) rather than exponential.

3. **Quantified constraints** — Evaluate whether Z3 forall/exists quantifiers are practical for simple array predicates like 'exists element > 0'.

4. **Loop summarization** — Research feasibility of inferring loop invariants for common patterns (filter, map, reduce with thresholds).

5. **Coverage-directed array mutation** — AFL-style splice/insert/delete mutations for array contents, complementing the existing fuzzing fallback.

Prerequisite: overlay_solved_values would need to reconstruct nested JSON from solved flat values for some of these approaches.

title: Extract branches from TypeScript AST in analyzer
description: The TS analyzer (shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts) currently returns an empty branches array. Implement branch extraction from if/else, switch/case, ternary, and logical &&/|| expressions using the TypeScript Compiler API. Each branch needs: id, line number, condition expression as string. This is a prerequisite for concolic execution — without branch info, the solver has nothing to target.

title: Staged pipeline: observe → analyze → solve → specify
description: Refactor the exploration pipeline into independent, composable stages: Stage 1 (Observe): Run function with many inputs (random, boundary, user-provided), record everything, no Z3. Stage 2 (Analyze): Build behavior map, identify uncovered branches, generate draft spec. Stage 3 (Solve): For uncovered branches, use Z3 to find triggering inputs, feed back to Stage 1. Stage 4 (Specify): Final specification with full coverage and derived properties. Each stage is independently useful and testable. Users get value from Stage 1+2 alone. This is an architectural refactor, not a rewrite — the pieces exist but are coupled.

title: Go frontend: omits functions field for files with no function definitions

**Requirement: Reproduction test first.** Before attempting any fix, write an automated test that reproduces the bug (demonstrating the deserialization failure for a file with no function definitions). The test must fail before the fix and pass after.

title: TS frontend: function scanner doesn't follow barrel re-exports
description:
Barrel files (index.ts with only 'export * from ./x' lines) are discovered as source files but yield zero functions. The scanner doesn't follow re-exports to find actual definitions. Affects remeda (150+ re-exports in index.ts) and any package using barrel patterns.

Design questions before implementation:
- How deep should re-export following go? (1 level? transitive?)
- How to handle circular re-exports?
- Should barrel files be excluded from discovery, or should re-exports be followed to source?
- How to handle external package re-exports (export * from 'lodash')?
- Performance impact of transitive resolution on large codebases?

Fix location: shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts — needs import graph walking at analysis time. This is a feature addition, not a simple bug fix.
design:
## Current Architecture

analyzer.ts uses ts.createProgram([absolutePath], ...) — it creates a TS program with a single root file and a TypeChecker (line 37-49). Re-export statements have no function bodies, so they yield nothing.

The core sends one file at a time via the Analyze protocol command. batch_analyze.rs iterates over discovered files and sends individual analyze requests. At line 190, function_entry_from_analysis(file_path.clone(), func) hardcodes the analyzed file as the source for ALL returned functions.

## Critical Protocol Gap

FunctionAnalysis has no source_file field. If the frontend follows re-exports and returns functions from other files, the core will:
1. Associate them with the barrel file's path
2. Send instrument/execute commands targeting the barrel file
3. Fail because the barrel file has no function bodies

This means we MUST either:
A. Add an optional source_file field to FunctionAnalysis and have batch_analyze respect it, OR
B. Have the frontend NOT return functions from other files — instead, skip barrel files entirely and rely on the core also discovering the actual source files

## Recommended Approach: Option B (simpler)

Don't follow re-exports at analysis time. Instead:

1. In the file discovery phase (core side), detect barrel files and resolve their re-exports to actual source files
2. Add the resolved source files to the file list (deduplicating)
3. Skip analyzing the barrel file itself (or let it return empty, which is fine)

This avoids protocol changes entirely. The core already discovers files; it just needs to also discover files referenced by re-exports.

**Problem**: the core doesn't understand TS module resolution. It discovers files by glob pattern (*.ts), not by following imports.

## Revised Recommended Approach: Option A (frontend reports source_file)

### Protocol Change
Add to FunctionAnalysis in protocol.rs:
```rust
/// Source file where this function is actually defined.
/// When present, overrides the file from the analyze request.
/// Used when re-exports are followed.
#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
pub source_file: Option<String>,
```

Add matching field to TS protocol.ts FunctionAnalysis interface.

### batch_analyze.rs Change
At line 190, use func.source_file as the file_path if present:
```rust
let actual_path = func.source_file.as_ref()
    .map(|p| PathBuf::from(p))
    .unwrap_or_else(|| file_path.clone());
entries.push(function_entry_from_analysis(actual_path, func));
```

Also update the qualified name construction at line 187 to use the actual path.

### analyzer.ts Change
After the normal forEachChild walk, if results is empty:
1. Check if file has ExportDeclaration nodes with moduleSpecifier
2. Use checker.getSymbolAtLocation(sourceFile) to get module symbol
3. Call checker.getExportsOfModule(moduleSymbol) to get all exported symbols
4. For each symbol, resolve to declaration via getDeclarations()
5. If declaration is FunctionDeclaration or ArrowFunction in another file, analyze it
6. Set source_file field to the actual declaration's file path

### Deduplication
When the core discovers both barrel.ts and actual-source.ts, functions may be analyzed twice. batch_analyze should deduplicate by qualified name (using actual source_file path).

### Scope & Constraints
- Only follow re-exports from files that yield zero functions from direct scanning
- Skip external package re-exports (declarations in node_modules)
- TypeChecker.getExportsOfModule() handles transitive/circular re-exports internally
- Re-export following must also handle named re-exports: export { foo } from './bar'

### Test Plan
- Protocol: round-trip test for FunctionAnalysis with source_file
- Frontend: fixture barrel.ts that re-exports from helper-a.ts and helper-b.ts
- Integration: verify functions from re-exports are instrumented/executed against correct file
- Dedup: verify same function isn't explored twice if discovered via barrel AND direct file scan

title: Wire pool into scan_orchestrator and CLI
description: Integration: (1) scan_orchestrator.rs: add pool: Option<Arc<InterestingPool>> to ScanConfig. Load from .shatter/seeds/pool.json at scan start. Pass to each explore_function call. Write back after all functions complete. Pool grows during scan — functions explored later in topological order benefit from earlier discoveries. (2) CLI: add --seeds-dir flag to explore and scan commands (defaults to .shatter/seeds/). Add --no-seeds flag to disable pool loading/saving. dependencies.title: Populate pool from exploration results
dependencies.description: After each function's exploration completes (in both explorer.rs random loop and orchestrator.rs concolic loop), harvest interesting inputs into the pool. Steps: (1) Iterate new_path_executions from ExplorationResult. (2) Classify each as error-triggering (use severity classifier) or rare-path (exercised by ≤2 inputs). (3) Decompose input vectors into individual (value, TypeInfo) pairs using the function's param types. Also store the full compound input if it's an Object/Array type. (4) Skip values already in boundary_dict (0, -1, empty string, etc. are tried everywhere already). (5) For existing pool entries matching (type, value), append new BehaviorObservation and update last_hit_epoch — this handles the case where a middling-interest value becomes highly interesting for a different function. (6) For new entries, insert and run eviction if bucket exceeds cap.

dependencies.title: Consume pool as seeds during exploration
dependencies.description: When starting exploration of a function, inject matching pool values as seed inputs. Steps: (1) Load pool once at scan start (scan_orchestrator holds Arc<InterestingPool> alongside existing Arc<BehaviorMapCache>). (2) For each function, for each parameter, find pool entries where pool_entry.type == param.typ. (3) For each match, construct single-value input vector: matching value at that param position, boundary defaults at other positions — same approach as literals_to_candidate_inputs in input_gen.rs. (4) In concolic mode (orchestrator.rs): inject as Seed priority worklist entries. In random mode (explorer.rs): prepend to iteration queue after literal candidates but before random generation. (5) Track which pool values triggered novel paths during this exploration to update hit_count/last_hit_epoch.

Extract from str-mxa3: wire the existing crypto registry (str-34xq) into frontend analysis pipelines and define the protocol message for reporting detected crypto boundaries to core.

1. Define a CryptoBoundary protocol message (or add to existing analyze response) that frontends use to report matched crypto calls
2. TS frontend: load crypto-registry.toml at startup, match call targets during analysis, report via protocol
3. Go frontend: same integration
4. Rust frontend: same integration (or stub if analyze is too early)
5. Integration test: analyze a file that calls crypto.createDecipheriv, verify the boundary is reported

This is the 'end-to-end validation' that str-9asc and str-tycq were waiting for. Once this works, Layers 2 and 3 can integrate using the same protocol message format.

dependencies.title: Crypto API registry for known library functions
dependencies.description:
Ship a static config file mapping known crypto function paths that frontends consult during analysis. This is Layer 1 of crypto function recognition (str-mxa3) — zero runtime cost, high precision.

Registry format: TOML or JSON, organized by language and package. Each entry specifies:
- Function path (e.g. crypto.createDecipheriv)
- Direction: encrypt | decrypt | both (for authenticated encryption)
- Parameter roles: which arg is key, which is data, which is IV/nonce
- Output semantics: plaintext or ciphertext

Initial coverage:
- Node.js: crypto module (createDecipheriv, privateDecrypt, subtle.decrypt), crypto-js (CryptoJS.AES.decrypt, CryptoJS.DES.decrypt, etc.), tweetnacl (secretbox.open), libsodium-wrappers
- Go: crypto/aes + crypto/cipher (NewCBCDecrypter, AEAD.Open/Seal, Stream XORKeyStream), x/crypto (chacha20poly1305, nacl/secretbox)
- Rust: aes-gcm (Aes256Gcm::decrypt), ring (aead::open_in_place, aead::seal_in_place), chacha20poly1305 crate

Frontends load the registry at startup and match against call targets during analysis/instrumentation. Matched calls get reported to the core engine via the crypto boundary protocol message.

The registry file lives in a well-known location (e.g. shatter-core/data/crypto-registry.toml) and is extensible by users via a local override file.

title: String fragment injection mutation operator
description: New mutation operator in mutate_string: insert a random fragment from the dictionary at a random position in the string. Gets domain-relevant characters like @ and . into strings without waiting for char-by-char luck. This is essentially AFL's dictionary mode applied at the string level. Depends on literal dictionary threading.

dependencies.title: Thread literal dictionary into mutate_string

The dictionary contains source-code literals extracted during analysis plus common delimiters (@, ., ://, /, etc.). title: Solver declares string params as Int — pass ParamInfo to Z3 variable creation
description:
Root cause of str-omrx. infer_sort() in solver.rs:162 defaults SymExpr::Param to Sort::Int. When a string param appears inside .length (which returns Int), the param gets declared as Int in Z3. Z3 then solves for an integer, overlay_solved_values writes it back as json!(42), and the frontend crashes with 'email.indexOf is not a function'.

Fix: thread &[ParamInfo] into solve_for_new_path → to_z3_expr. When creating a Z3 variable for SymExpr::Param, look up the param name in ParamInfo and use its TypeInfo to select the correct Sort (Str→Sort::Str, Int→Sort::Int, Float→Sort::Real, Bool→Sort::Bool). Fall back to infer_sort only for sub-expressions like object paths that aren't top-level params.

Key files: solver.rs (infer_sort, to_z3_expr, solve_for_new_path), orchestrator.rs (calls solve_for_new_path).

Reproduction test already written: e2e_concolic.rs::concolic_validateemail_boundary_seeds_only

title: Epic: Revalidation and regression detection

Distinguishes genuine regressions from expected drift (code changed), flaky behavior, and nondeterministic noise. Integrates with nondeterminism mask to avoid false positives. Provides CLI command and CI-ready JSON output with nonzero exit codes.

title: Improve shatter explore output readability with color, layout, and visual hierarchy
description:
The current explore output is functional but visually flat and hard to scan. It needs:

**Current problems:**
- No color — everything is monochrome terminal text
- No visual separation between functions — just a blank line
- Path clusters blend together with no visual anchoring
- Coverage metrics are buried at the bottom with no visual weight
- Long argument strings (e.g. JSON objects) get truncated mid-line awkwardly
- No summary header or footer for multi-function runs

**Proposed improvements:**

1. **Color coding** (via ANSI escape codes, respecting NO_COLOR/TERM):
   - Function names in bold/bright
   - Return values in green, thrown errors in red/yellow
   - Coverage percentages: green ≥80%, yellow 50-79%, red <50%
   - Cluster numbers in cyan/bold
   - Dim/gray for less important details (constraint counts)

2. **Layout & structure**:
   - Box-drawing or rule lines between functions (e.g. ─── or ═══)
   - Indented tree-style for path clusters with │ ├ └ connectors
   - Summary banner at top: 'Exploring 4 functions...' and footer with totals
   - Coverage bar: visual [████░░░░] 58% instead of just text

3. **ASCII art / visual elements**:
   - Mini progress/coverage bars per function
   - Status icons: ✓ for good coverage, ⚠ for partial, ✗ for low
   - Error paths get a distinct marker (⚡ or 💥 or red ✗)

4. **Argument formatting**:
   - Truncate long JSON args intelligently (show key structure, elide values)
   - Align example calls for visual scanning

5. **Respect terminal conventions**:
   - NO_COLOR env var disables all ANSI codes
   - --no-color CLI flag
   - Detect non-TTY (piped output) and auto-disable color
   - --color=always/auto/never flag

**Reference output (example of what it could look like):**
```
═══ Shatter Explore ═══  4 functions

── classifyNumber (01-arithmetic.ts:10) ──────────────
  4 paths · 7/12 lines (58%) [████▌···] ⚠

  ├─ 1. returns "zero"
  │     classifyNumber(0.0) → "zero"
  ├─ 2. returns "positive-even"
  │     classifyNumber(2.0) → "positive-even"
  ├─ 3. returns "positive-odd"
  │     classifyNumber(0.5) → "positive-odd"
  └─ 4. returns "negative"
        classifyNumber(-23.0) → "negative"

  Branches: 3/3 (100%) ✓  Constraints: 203/203 (100%) ✓

── safeDivide (04-errors.ts:10) ──────────────────────
  3 paths · 7/13 lines (54%) [████····] ⚠

  ├─ 1. ⚡ throws Error: division by zero [validation]
  │     safeDivide(0.0, 0.0) → Error
  ├─ 2. returns -0.0294...
  │     safeDivide(1.0, -34.0) → -0.0294
  └─ 3. returns 0
        safeDivide(0.0, 0.5) → 0

  Branches: 3/3 (100%) ✓  Constraints: 278/278 (100%) ✓

═══ Summary: 15 paths across 4 functions ═══
```

**Implementation location:** `format_exploration_report()` in `shatter-core/src/explorer.rs` and the CLI display code in `shatter-cli/src/main.rs`.

Consider using the `owo-colors` or `colored` crate for ANSI color support, and `unicode-width` for proper terminal width handling.

title: Implement adaptive MetaStrategy with outcome-based scoring
description: Meta-strategy that selects among registered InputStrategies using adaptive scoring. Core algorithm: each strategy's score is its hit rate (new paths / total candidates) over a sliding window of its last 100 outcomes. Cold-start protection: strategies with fewer than min(20, estimated_size) candidates supplied get the average score of graduated strategies (or 1.0 if all are in cold start), preventing premature deprioritization. Floor: every non-exhausted strategy gets at least 2% allocation (configurable, range 1-5%). When a strategy exhausts, its allocation is redistributed uniformly to all remaining non-exhausted strategies. Selection is weighted random sampling from normalized scores. Config struct: MetaConfig { window_size: 100, cold_start_threshold: 20, floor: 0.02, adaptive: true, static_weights: Option<Vec<(String, f64)>> }. When static_weights is set, uses fixed distribution instead of adaptive (still redistributes uniformly on exhaustion). When adaptive is false and no static_weights, uses pure round-robin.

dependencies.title: Implement BoundarySeeds strategy with neighbor enumeration
dependencies.description: Exhaustible strategy that enumerates type-aware boundary values and their neighbors. Int: 0, ±1, ±2, ±3, powers of two (4,8,16,32,64,128,256,1024) with ±1 neighbors, MAX, MAX-1, MIN, MIN+1 (~36 values per param). Float: 0.0, ±1.0, ±0.5, ±2.0, π, -π, e, -e, π/2, π/4, 0.1, 0.3 (IEEE 754 trap), ±1e-10, ±1e10, NaN, ±Inf, MIN_POSITIVE, MAX, (1<<53) precision boundary. String: empty, space, single char, two chars, long string (300 chars), tab, newline, CRLF, null byte, embedded null, ANSI escape, multibyte unicode (é, 日本語, 🎉), stringified primitives (true, false, null, 0, -1), injection canaries. Bool: true, false. For multi-param functions, use pairwise coverage (not full cartesian product) — vary one param while holding others at neutral defaults (0, empty string, false), then rotate. This caps candidates at ~36*N instead of 36^N.

dependencies.title: Extract Z3 constraint solving into Z3Solver strategy
dependencies.description: Reactive strategy that produces inputs by negating path constraints. feedback() records symbolic constraints from new paths. next() yields from internal queue of solved inputs. Infinite (produces work while unsolved constraints exist), but may have empty queue between feedback cycles. Wraps existing logic from orchestrator.rs lines 395-424.

title: Replace naive fuzz_inputs with type-aware mutation from input_gen.rs
description: The orchestrator's fuzz_inputs() (orchestrator.rs:467-511) is a hardcoded naive fuzzer: flip bools, ±1 for numbers, empty/'a' for strings. It doesn't know parameter types and can't mutate arrays, objects, unions, or nullable types. Meanwhile input_gen.rs has type-aware mutate_inputs() (line 946) that handles all TypeInfo variants with biased distributions, plus crossover_inputs() (line 1251) for recombining two parent inputs. These are tested but never called from the orchestrator. Replace fuzz_inputs() with mutate_inputs() using the function's ParamInfo from analysis, and add crossover between high-performing parent inputs once fitness scoring is wired in (str-hoyl).
notes: Key insight: fuzz_inputs() is ONLY called for Unknown constraints (orchestrator.rs:428-441) — branches Z3 can't solve. The naive fuzzer is worst exactly where good fuzzing matters most. Opaque/unknown branches are the ones that need type-aware mutation and crossover the most, since they can't be solved symbolically.

dependencies.title: Wire genetic fitness scoring into orchestrator worklist ordering
dependencies.description: The orchestrator (orchestrator.rs) uses a BinaryHeap ordered solely by InputSource (UserProvided > Z3Solved > Fuzzed > Seed). Two Z3Solved inputs are treated identically regardless of whether the parent execution reached depth 2 or depth 15. The genetic_fitness module exists with tested scoring logic but is never imported or called outside its own tests. Wire it in: when an execution produces new worklist entries (via Z3 solving or fuzzing), tag those entries with the parent execution's fitness score (including depth). Use fitness-informed ordering so the orchestrator spends its budget on inputs derived from deeper, higher-coverage executions rather than treating all same-source inputs equally.
dependencies.notes: Design note: orchestrator's covered_paths: HashSet<u64> (line 306) and FitnessContext.seen_paths: HashSet<u64> (genetic_fitness.rs:54) both track path hashes. When wiring fitness in, share state rather than duplicate. Also: WorklistEntry Ord impl (line 139-143) only compares source field — this is the specific code that needs to change to incorporate fitness.

dependencies.title: Epic: Assemble genetic search pipeline in orchestrator
dependencies.description:
Three sophisticated modules exist but are completely disconnected from the exploration loop:

1. genetic_fitness.rs — 4-axis fitness scoring (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty), tested, never called outside tests
2. strategy.rs — InputStrategy trait + MetaStrategy with adaptive selection, feedback, hit-rate scoring, tested, never imported by orchestrator
3. input_gen.rs — type-aware mutate_inputs() and crossover_inputs(), tested, never called by orchestrator (which uses a naive hardcoded fuzz_inputs instead)

The orchestrator uses a BinaryHeap ordered solely by InputSource enum and a 30-line hardcoded fuzzer. Assembling these into a genetic search pipeline means: (a) fitness-scored worklist ordering so deeper/higher-coverage executions get priority, (b) adaptive strategy selection so budget flows to whichever generation method works best, (c) type-aware mutation and crossover between high-fitness parents. This is the difference between directed exploration and random walking.

Sub-issues: str-yvp4 (depth axis), str-hoyl (fitness→worklist), str-lebv (MetaStrategy integration), plus naive fuzzer replacement.

title: String-level crossover operators for genetic search
description:
The existing crossover_inputs() (str-olf) operates at parameter level and array-element level — it picks whole values from parent A or B. For string parameters, it never combines characters from both parents.

String-level crossover strategies needed:
- Splice crossover: prefix from parent A + suffix from parent B (e.g. 'test@' + 'domain.com' → 'test@domain.com')
- Substring insertion: insert a substring from one parent into the other
- Single-point crossover: split both strings at a random position, swap tails

This naturally solves the literal composition problem (str-omrx context): if parent A is '@' (from a literal seed) and parent B is 'hello' (from boundary), crossover produces 'hel@lo' or '@hello' — strings containing '@' with length > 0.

Should be added to crossover_inputs() in input_gen.rs, gated on the parameter being TypeInfo::Str.

Parent: str-oi1d (genetic search pipeline epic)

dependencies.title: Type-aware crossover operators for genetic search
dependencies.description: Add public crossover_inputs() function to shatter-core/src/input_gen.rs. Takes two parent Vec<Value> and produces one or two children. Strategy: uniform crossover at the parameter level (each child param randomly selected from parent A or B). For object params, field-level crossover. For array params, single-point crossover on elements. crossover_rate parameter controls probability of crossover vs cloning parent. Unit tests verifying children have valid types and mix parent values. Parent: str-iiz.

The ExploreConfig struct in explorer.rs has no field for candidate inputs, and the input generation loop (explorer.rs:343-386) only uses literal seeds, pool seeds, custom generators, and random — never user-provided candidates. Fix: add candidate_inputs field to ExploreConfig, convert and pass them in main.rs, insert them into the input priority chain between pool seeds and generators.
dependencies.close_reason: Fixed in commit 0f656e7 — user_seeds field added to ExploreConfig, wired in main.rs

title: Epic: Assemble genetic search pipeline in orchestrator
description:
Three sophisticated modules exist but are completely disconnected from the exploration loop:

1. genetic_fitness.rs — 4-axis fitness scoring (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty), tested, never called outside tests
2. strategy.rs — InputStrategy trait + MetaStrategy with adaptive selection, feedback, hit-rate scoring, tested, never imported by orchestrator
3. input_gen.rs — type-aware mutate_inputs() and crossover_inputs(), tested, never called by orchestrator (which uses a naive hardcoded fuzz_inputs instead)

The orchestrator uses a BinaryHeap ordered solely by InputSource enum and a 30-line hardcoded fuzzer. Assembling these into a genetic search pipeline means: (a) fitness-scored worklist ordering so deeper/higher-coverage executions get priority, (b) adaptive strategy selection so budget flows to whichever generation method works best, (c) type-aware mutation and crossover between high-fitness parents. This is the difference between directed exploration and random walking.

Sub-issues: str-yvp4 (depth axis), str-hoyl (fitness→worklist), str-lebv (MetaStrategy integration), plus naive fuzzer replacement.

dependencies.title: String-level crossover operators for genetic search
dependencies.description:
The existing crossover_inputs() (str-olf) operates at parameter level and array-element level — it picks whole values from parent A or B. For string parameters, it never combines characters from both parents.

String-level crossover strategies needed:
- Splice crossover: prefix from parent A + suffix from parent B (e.g. 'test@' + 'domain.com' → 'test@domain.com')
- Substring insertion: insert a substring from one parent into the other
- Single-point crossover: split both strings at a random position, swap tails

This naturally solves the literal composition problem (str-omrx context): if parent A is '@' (from a literal seed) and parent B is 'hello' (from boundary), crossover produces 'hel@lo' or '@hello' — strings containing '@' with length > 0.

Should be added to crossover_inputs() in input_gen.rs, gated on the parameter being TypeInfo::Str.

Parent: str-oi1d (genetic search pipeline epic)

title: Concolic explorer stuck on single path when initial execution yields no constraints
description: When the concolic explorer's first execution returns empty path_constraints (e.g. validateEmail('') hits 'email.length === 0' and returns immediately with zero branch decisions), the solver has nothing to negate and exploration terminates after one iteration. The orchestrator should fall back to using static branch conditions from the analyze phase as a constraint skeleton, or use literal/boundary seeds to reach deeper code paths before starting the concolic loop. Currently it tries boundary value seeds (empty string, 0, true, etc.) but none of these contain '@' so they all hit the same first two branches. The concolic explorer needs a seeding strategy that uses structural information from the analyzed branches (e.g. literals like '@' appearing in indexOf conditions) to construct inputs that reach past guard clauses.
notes: Reproduced in e2e_concolic.rs::concolic_validateemail_boundary_seeds_only. Root cause: solver declares string params as Int (str-6ayh). Secondary: concolic path missing literal seeds (str-bb5a). Related improvements: destructuring (str-90ws), closure mutable capture (str-mu3h).

dependencies.title: Solver declares string params as Int — pass ParamInfo to Z3 variable creation
dependencies.description:
Root cause of str-omrx. infer_sort() in solver.rs:162 defaults SymExpr::Param to Sort::Int. When a string param appears inside .length (which returns Int), the param gets declared as Int in Z3. Z3 then solves for an integer, overlay_solved_values writes it back as json!(42), and the frontend crashes with 'email.indexOf is not a function'.

Fix: thread &[ParamInfo] into solve_for_new_path → to_z3_expr. When creating a Z3 variable for SymExpr::Param, look up the param name in ParamInfo and use its TypeInfo to select the correct Sort (Str→Sort::Str, Int→Sort::Int, Float→Sort::Real, Bool→Sort::Bool). Fall back to infer_sort only for sub-expressions like object paths that aren't top-level params.

Key files: solver.rs (infer_sort, to_z3_expr, solve_for_new_path), orchestrator.rs (calls solve_for_new_path).

Reproduction test already written: e2e_concolic.rs::concolic_validateemail_boundary_seeds_only

dependencies.title: CLI concolic path doesn't pass literal-derived seeds to orchestrator
dependencies.description:
The CLI concolic path (main.rs:1121-1149) seeds the orchestrator with only generate_boundary_inputs() + pool + user inputs. The random explorer (explorer.rs:589-632) uses literals_to_candidate_inputs() from the analyze phase, but the concolic path never calls it.

Fix: add literal_candidates = literals_to_candidate_inputs(&func.params, &func.literals) and extend seed_inputs in main.rs after line 1123. This gives the concolic loop access to string literals like '@', '.', 'express' extracted during static analysis.

This is a 2-line fix but secondary to str-6ayh — even with literal seeds, the solver would still produce wrong-type values without the ParamInfo fix.

title: Structure-aware string mutation algorithm
description: A string-specific mutation algorithm distinct from byte-level and existing char-level: multi-point character substitution, regex-pattern-guided insertion, dictionary-weighted character selection, unicode-aware operations. Complements byte-level mutation (for binary) and existing single-char mutation (too granular for structural patterns). Independent.

dependencies.title: Epic: Enhanced mutation operators for strings and binary
dependencies.description: Strengthen the mutation pipeline with string fragment injection from source literals, substring duplication, havoc mode (compound mutations), structure-aware string mutation, and byte-level mutation for binary buffers. Addresses the gap where the GA's string handling is weakest — character-level perturbation is too sparse for discovering structural patterns like emails or URLs.

title: Thread literal dictionary into mutate_string
description: Add a dictionary: &[&str] parameter to mutate_string() in input_gen.rs. The dictionary contains source-code literals extracted during analysis plus common delimiters (@, ., ://, /, etc.). Currently mutate_string has no access to domain-relevant fragments — it only does random character operations. This is the foundation for fragment injection. ~20 LoC change.

title: Support time-based exploration limits (--timeout-explore)
description: Currently exploration is bounded by the number of test inputs (--max-inputs). Add a --timeout-explore flag that caps exploration wall-clock time per function. This is more intuitive for users who want to say 'spend 30 seconds per function' rather than guessing an input count. Should work for both random explorer and concolic orchestrator. Note: --timeout-total already exists (parsed but unused) — this is per-function.

title: Unify explorer and orchestrator into single strategy-driven loop
description: Replace the two separate exploration loops (explorer::explore_function with its if-else chain and orchestrator::explore with its BinaryHeap worklist) with a single loop that polls the MetaStrategy. The random explorer path becomes MetaStrategy with [UserProvided, Literals, PoolSeeds, BoundarySeeds, Random]. The concolic explorer path becomes MetaStrategy with [UserProvided, BoundarySeeds, Z3Solver, Fuzzer]. The CLI --concolic flag (or auto-detection of constraint support) selects the strategy set. Single loop: execute → compute path hash → fan out feedback to all strategies → check termination (plateau, budget, all exhausted).

dependencies.title: Input strategy decomposition and adaptive meta-strategy
dependencies.description: Replace the ad-hoc if-else input generation chain in explorer.rs and the separate worklist model in orchestrator.rs with a unified InputStrategy trait and adaptive meta-strategy. Each input source becomes a composable strategy struct with a next()/feedback() interface. A meta-strategy selects among strategies using outcome-based adaptive scoring with configurable cold-start protection, per-strategy floor allocation, and optional user-specified static weights. This unifies the two explorer paths under a single loop with different strategy sets.

dependencies.title: Implement adaptive MetaStrategy with outcome-based scoring
dependencies.description: Meta-strategy that selects among registered InputStrategies using adaptive scoring. Core algorithm: each strategy's score is its hit rate (new paths / total candidates) over a sliding window of its last 100 outcomes. Cold-start protection: strategies with fewer than min(20, estimated_size) candidates supplied get the average score of graduated strategies (or 1.0 if all are in cold start), preventing premature deprioritization. Floor: every non-exhausted strategy gets at least 2% allocation (configurable, range 1-5%). When a strategy exhausts, its allocation is redistributed uniformly to all remaining non-exhausted strategies. Selection is weighted random sampling from normalized scores. Config struct: MetaConfig { window_size: 100, cold_start_threshold: 20, floor: 0.02, adaptive: true, static_weights: Option<Vec<(String, f64)>> }. When static_weights is set, uses fixed distribution instead of adaptive (still redistributes uniformly on exhaustion). When adaptive is false and no static_weights, uses pure round-robin.

title: User-declared nondeterminism in .shatter/config.yaml
description:
Add nondeterminism section to ShatterConfig (config.rs) with confirmed and rejected lists. Format:

nondeterminism:
  confirmed:
    - function: createUser
      path: '$.id'
      reason: 'UUID generated per call'
  rejected:
    - function: processOrder
      path: '$.orderId'
      reason: 'deterministic sequence from input'

Confirmed entries become UserDeclared evidence (highest precedence). Rejected entries suppress re-flagging by heuristics so the tool doesn't keep asking about known-deterministic fields.

title: Branch-parameter attribution: extract param names from SymExpr constraints
description: Walk SymExpr trees to extract which Param names appear in each branch's symbolic constraint. New function extract_param_names(expr: &SymExpr) -> HashSet<String> in sym_expr.rs or a new attribution module. This tells us which parameters are involved in each branch condition, enabling targeted parameter drilling. Independent — no dependencies.

dependencies.title: Epic: Multi-frontier exploration with parameter drilling
dependencies.description: Track multiple frontier branches per function, attribute blocking parameters via symbolic constraint analysis, and apply targeted parameter drilling (pin non-blocking params, intensively mutate the blocking one). Includes stall detection and coverage-guided frontier prioritization. See docs/GLOSSARY.md for terminology.

title: Source-agnostic harvesting: ensure all input sources reach the pool
description: Verify and fix that all execution sources are harvested when they trigger interesting behavior: concolic-solved values, random exploration, fuzzed inputs, user-provided seeds, cross-function pool seeds. The pool should not care about input provenance — a value is interesting because of what it does, not where it came from. Audit current gaps and close them.

dependencies.title: Harvest from concolic orchestrator: wire pool save in concolic path
dependencies.description: Bug: orchestrator.rs explore() does not call harvest_from_exploration(). The CLI concolic path loads the pool for seeds but never writes back discovered interesting inputs. Wire harvest_from_exploration() call after concolic exploration completes, and save_pool() in the CLI concolic path. ~30 LoC. Related to str-oi1d (orchestrator modernization).

title: Scan report uses absolute file paths instead of relative
description: shatter-report/scan-report.json contains absolute paths like /home/ketan/project/shatter/examples/standalone/ts/01-arithmetic.ts. These should be relative paths (e.g. examples/standalone/ts/01-arithmetic.ts) for portability — the report breaks when shared or moved to a different machine. The scan output code that populates file_path needs to relativize paths against the project root.

title: Symbolic triage: use accumulated constraints to reject uninteresting inputs without execution
description:
When the concolic solver extracts path constraints (e.g. x > 10 && str.contains(s, "@")), we can evaluate those constraints against candidate inputs statically to predict which branch path they would take. If the predicted path is already in seen_paths, discard the input without executing it. This avoids the cost of spawning frontend execution for inputs that are overwhelmingly likely to be redundant.

Implementation sketch:
1. After each execution round, collect the SymConstraint entries from branch_path into a constraint index keyed by branch_id
2. For each candidate input, evaluate known constraints to predict the branch decisions
3. If all predicted branches map to an already-seen path hash, skip execution (fast path)
4. Periodically execute 'predicted-uninteresting' inputs anyway to catch constraint model errors (e.g. 1-in-20 sampling)

Constraints come from BranchDecision.constraint: SymConstraint in explorer.rs. Currently only used for Z3 solving, not for pre-filtering.

Limitations:
- Only works for branches with captured SymConstraints (Unknown constraints can't be evaluated)
- Cannot predict side effects, exceptions, or loop iteration counts
- Need a fallback sampling rate to catch model drift

Even a 50% fast-rejection rate would effectively double throughput of long farming runs.

title: Add execution depth axis to genetic fitness function
description: The genetic_fitness module scores executions on 4 axes (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty) but none capture how deep into nested control flow an input penetrates. An input reaching branch depth 15 is strictly more valuable than one reaching depth 2 — it's closer to exploring the deepest, hardest-to-reach behaviors. Add a 5th 'depth' component: normalize branch_path.len() via asymptotic formula (len / (len + k)) and add it to FitnessWeights/FitnessBreakdown. This is a prerequisite for wiring fitness into the orchestrator.

title: Z3 solver timeout
description: Both solve_for_new_path and solve_constraints in solver.rs create Config::new() with no params. Z3 crate 0.19 provides Config::set_timeout_msec(ms) — context-level timeout. When Z3 hits deadline, solver.check() returns SatResult::Unknown with reason 'timeout'; check_and_extract already handles this as SolverError::Unknown. Add solver_timeout_ms: Option<u64> param to both solve functions. Apply cfg.set_timeout_msec(ms) when Some. Add solver_timeout_ms to ExploreConfig (explorer.rs) and thread through orchestrator.rs:396. Add --solver-timeout CLI flag (optional, seconds) to Explore/Scan/Run. Add test: 1ms timeout on satisfiable constraint → either Sat or Unknown (no panic).

title: Havoc mode: compound multi-mutation sequences
description: New mutation operator that applies 2-5 random mutations in sequence (inspired by AFL's havoc stage). Compound mutations find deeper paths than single edits. Applicable to all types, not just strings. For strings, composes fragment injection, char substitution, substring duplication, and crossover operations. Depends on fragment injection and str-ek5g (string crossover).

dependencies.title: String fragment injection mutation operator
dependencies.description: New mutation operator in mutate_string: insert a random fragment from the dictionary at a random position in the string. Gets domain-relevant characters like @ and . into strings without waiting for char-by-char luck. This is essentially AFL's dictionary mode applied at the string level. Depends on literal dictionary threading.

title: Restructure orchestrator::explore() to separate observation from solving
description: Break the tight concolic loop in orchestrator::explore() into separate Observe and Solve/Generate phases. Observe: execute from all input sources, collect results. Solve/Generate: extract constraints from results, call Z3 for uncovered branches, produce new candidate inputs. The outer loop iterates: Observe round → Solve/Generate → feed new candidates → next Observe round. This creates the seam for the future pluggable generator trait (str-4bx). Define a SolveOutput or GenerateOutput type for what the solve phase produces.

dependencies.title: Rename ExplorationResult → ObservationOutput, unify IR types
dependencies.description: Rename ExplorationResult to ObservationOutput as the single canonical pipeline type. Fix orchestrator::ExploreResult to include function_name and total_lines so the From conversion doesn't need caller patching. Delete the ObservationOutput adapter struct and From<ExplorationResult>/From<ExploreResult> conversions in pipeline.rs. Add Serialize/Deserialize derives for JSON caching. Use a type alias during transition to limit blast radius. Update all consumers: spec.rs (build_spec), scan_orchestrator.rs, main.rs, explorer.rs.

title: explore accepts file-level targets
description: Add bare file path support

title: Hybrid concolic + coverage-guided fuzzing
description:
For code paths guarded by opaque transformations (encryption, hashing, compression, serialization), symbolic reasoning alone may be intractable. A hybrid approach combines coverage-guided fuzzing with concolic execution:

1. Fuzzer generates inputs and runs them, collecting branch coverage
2. When the fuzzer gets stuck (no new coverage for N iterations), hand off to concolic solver
3. Concolic solver identifies path constraints at the frontier and generates targeted inputs
4. New inputs feed back into the fuzzer corpus

This is the approach used by Driller (AFL + angr) and QSYM. For Shatter, the frontends already provide execution + coverage — the new component is a lightweight fuzzer loop in the core engine that coordinates with the existing concolic explorer.

Key design decisions:
- Mutation strategy: byte-level random vs structure-aware (leverage type info from frontends)
- Handoff heuristic: coverage plateau detection
- Corpus management: shared between fuzzer and solver
- Integration point: orchestrator layer, alongside existing multi-round exploration

This is a significant new subsystem. Start with a design doc before implementation.
